[PAGE 7]
[L s=21.2 r=1.42 x=197 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Translator’s Preface
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=163 b=0 i=0 f=f3] A few words about Dostoevsky himself may help the English reader to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=181 b=0 i=0 f=f3] understand his work.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=199 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dostoevsky was the son of a doctor. His parents were very hard-
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=217 b=0 i=0 f=f3] working and deeply religious people, but so poor that they lived with their
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=235 b=0 i=0 f=f3] five children in only two rooms. The father and mother spent their evenings
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=253 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in reading aloud to their children, generally from books of a serious
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=271 b=0 i=0 f=f3] character.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=289 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Though always sickly and delicate Dostoevsky came out third in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=307 b=0 i=0 f=f3] final examination of the Petersburg school of Engineering. There he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=325 b=0 i=0 f=f3] already begun his first work, “Poor Folk.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=343 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This story was published by the poet Nekrassov in his review and was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=361 b=0 i=0 f=f3] received with acclamations. The shy, unknown youth found himself
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=379 b=0 i=0 f=f3] instantly something of a celebrity. A brilliant and successful career seemed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=397 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to open before him, but those hopes were soon dashed. In 1849 he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=415 b=0 i=0 f=f3] arrested.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=433 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Though neither by temperament nor conviction a revolutionist,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=451 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dostoevsky was one of a little group of young men who met together to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=469 b=0 i=0 f=f3] read Fourier and Proudhon. He was accused of “taking part in conversations
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=487 b=0 i=0 f=f3] against the censorship, of reading a letter from Byelinsky to Gogol, and of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=505 b=0 i=0 f=f3] knowing of the intention to set up a printing press.” Under Nicholas I. (that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=523 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “stern and just man,” as Maurice Baring calls him) this was enough, and he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=541 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was condemned to death. After eight months’ imprisonment he was with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=559 b=0 i=0 f=f3] twenty-one others taken out to the Semyonovsky Square to be shot. Writing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=577 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to his brother Mihail, Dostoevsky says: “They snapped words over our
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=595 b=0 i=0 f=f3] heads, and they made us put on the white shirts worn by persons
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=613 b=0 i=0 f=f3] condemned to death. Thereupon we were bound in threes to stakes, to suffer
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=631 b=0 i=0 f=f3] execution. Being the third in the row, I concluded I had only a few minutes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=649 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of life before me. I thought of you and your dear ones and I contrived to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=667 b=0 i=0 f=f3] kiss Plestcheiev and Dourov, who were next to me, and to bid them
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=685 b=0 i=0 f=f3] farewell. Suddenly the troops beat a tattoo, we were unbound, brought back

[PAGE 8]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] upon the scaffold, and informed that his Majesty had spared us our lives.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The sentence was commuted to hard labour.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] One of the prisoners, Grigoryev, went mad as soon as he was untied,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and never regained his sanity.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The intense suffering of this experience left a lasting stamp on
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Dostoevsky’s mind. Though his religious temper led him in the end to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accept every suffering with resignation and to regard it as a blessing in his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] own case, he constantly recurs to the subject in his writings. He describes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the awful agony of the condemned man and insists on the cruelty of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inflicting such torture. Then followed four years of penal servitude, spent in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the company of common criminals in Siberia, where he began the “Dead
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] House,” and some years of service in a disciplinary battalion.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had shown signs of some obscure nervous disease before his arrest
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and this now developed into violent attacks of epilepsy, from which he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] suffered for the rest of his life. The fits occurred three or four times a year
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and were more frequent in periods of great strain. In 1859 he was allowed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to return to Russia. He started a journal—“Vremya,” which was forbidden
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] by the Censorship through a misunderstanding. In 1864 he lost his first wife
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and his brother Mihail. He was in terrible poverty, yet he took upon himself
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the payment of his brother’s debts. He started another journal—“The
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Epoch,” which within a few months was also prohibited. He was weighed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] down by debt, his brother’s family was dependent on him, he was forced to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] write at heart-breaking speed, and is said never to have corrected his work.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The later years of his life were much softened by the tenderness and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] devotion of his second wife.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] In June 1880 he made his famous speech at the unveiling of the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] monument to Pushkin in Moscow and he was received with extraordinary
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] demonstrations of love and honour.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] A few months later Dostoevsky died. He was followed to the grave by a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] vast multitude of mourners, who “gave the hapless man the funeral of a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] king.” He is still probably the most widely read writer in Russia.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] In the words of a Russian critic, who seeks to explain the feeling
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inspired by Dostoevsky: “He was one of ourselves, a man of our blood and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] our bone, but one who has suffered and has seen so much more deeply than
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] we have his insight impresses us as wisdom… that wisdom of the heart
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which we seek that we may learn from it how to live. All his other gifts

[PAGE 9]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] came to him from nature, this he won for himself and through it he became
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] great.”

[PAGE 10]
[L s=27.5 r=1.00 x=267 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Part I

[PAGE 11]
[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=223 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter One
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] O
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=297 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] early in July a young man came out of
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=97 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] N AN EXCEPTIONALLY HOT EVENING
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the garret in which he lodged in S. Place and walked slowly, as though in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hesitation, towards K. bridge.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had successfully avoided meeting his landlady on the staircase. His
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] garret was under the roof of a high, five-storied house and was more like a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cupboard than a room. The landlady who provided him with garret, dinners,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and attendance, lived on the floor below, and every time he went out he was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] obliged to pass her kitchen, the door of which invariably stood open. And
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] each time he passed, the young man had a sick, frightened feeling, which
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] made him scowl and feel ashamed. He was hopelessly in debt to his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] landlady, and was afraid of meeting her.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This was not because he was cowardly and abject, quite the contrary;
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] but for some time past he had been in an overstrained irritable condition,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] verging on hypochondria. He had become so completely absorbed in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] himself, and isolated from his fellows that he dreaded meeting, not only his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] landlady, but anyone at all. He was crushed by poverty, but the anxieties of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his position had of late ceased to weigh upon him. He had given up
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] attending to matters of practical importance; he had lost all desire to do so.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Nothing that any landlady could do had a real terror for him. But to be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stopped on the stairs, to be forced to listen to her trivial, irrelevant gossip, to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pestering demands for payment, threats and complaints, and to rack his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] brains for excuses, to prevaricate, to lie—no, rather than that, he would
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] creep down the stairs like a cat and slip out unseen.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This evening, however, on coming out into the street, he became acutely
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] aware of his fears.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I want to attempt a thing like that and am frightened by these trifles,”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he thought, with an odd smile. “Hm… yes, all is in a man’s hands and he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=669 b=0 i=0 f=f3] lets it all slip from cowardice, that’s an axiom. It would be interesting to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=687 b=0 i=0 f=f3] know what it is men are most afraid of. Taking a new step, uttering a new

[PAGE 12]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] word is what they fear most…. But I am talking too much. It’s because I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] chatter that I do nothing. Or perhaps it is that I chatter because I do nothing.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] I’ve learned to chatter this last month, lying for days together in my den
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] thinking… of Jack the Giant-killer. Why am I going there now? Am I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] capable of that? Is that serious? It is not serious at all. It’s simply a fantasy
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to amuse myself; a plaything! Yes, maybe it is a plaything.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The heat in the street was terrible: and the airlessness, the bustle and the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] plaster, scaffolding, bricks, and dust all about him, and that special
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petersburg stench, so familiar to all who are unable to get out of town in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] summer—all worked painfully upon the young man’s already overwrought
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] nerves. The insufferable stench from the pot-houses, which are particularly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] numerous in that part of the town, and the drunken men whom he met
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] continually, although it was a working day, completed the revolting misery
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of the picture. An expression of the profoundest disgust gleamed for a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] moment in the young man’s refined face. He was, by the way, exceptionally
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] handsome, above the average in height, slim, well-built, with beautiful dark
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] eyes and dark brown hair. Soon he sank into deep thought, or more
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accurately speaking into a complete blankness of mind; he walked along not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] observing what was about him and not caring to observe it. From time to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] time, he would mutter something, from the habit of talking to himself, to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which he had just confessed. At these moments he would become conscious
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that his ideas were sometimes in a tangle and that he was very weak; for
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] two days he had scarcely tasted food.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He was so badly dressed that even a man accustomed to shabbiness
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would have been ashamed to be seen in the street in such rags. In that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] quarter of the town, however, scarcely any shortcoming in dress would have
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] created surprise. Owing to the proximity of the Hay Market, the number of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] establishments of bad character, the preponderance of the trading and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] working class population crowded in these streets and alleys in the heart of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Petersburg, types so various were to be seen in the streets that no figure,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] however queer, would have caused surprise. But there was such
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accumulated bitterness and contempt in the young man’s heart, that, in spite
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of all the fastidiousness of youth, he minded his rags least of all in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] street. It was a different matter when he met with acquaintances or with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] former fellow students, whom, indeed, he disliked meeting at any time. And
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] yet when a drunken man who, for some unknown reason, was being taken

[PAGE 13]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] somewhere in a huge waggon dragged by a heavy dray horse, suddenly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] shouted at him as he drove past: “Hey there, German hatter” bawling at the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] top of his voice and pointing at him—the young man stopped suddenly and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] clutched tremulously at his hat. It was a tall round hat from Zimmerman’s,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] but completely worn out, rusty with age, all torn and bespattered, brimless
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and bent on one side in a most unseemly fashion. Not shame, however, but
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] quite another feeling akin to terror had overtaken him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I knew it,” he muttered in confusion, “I thought so! That’s the worst of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] all! Why, a stupid thing like this, the most trivial detail might spoil the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] whole plan. Yes, my hat is too noticeable…. It looks absurd and that makes
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] it noticeable…. With my rags I ought to wear a cap, any sort of old
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pancake, but not this grotesque thing. Nobody wears such a hat, it would be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] noticed a mile off, it would be remembered…. What matters is that people
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would remember it, and that would give them a clue. For this business one
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] should be as little conspicuous as possible…. Trifles, trifles are what
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] matter! Why, it’s just such trifles that always ruin everything….”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He had not far to go; he knew indeed how many steps it was from the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] gate of his lodging house: exactly seven hundred and thirty. He had counted
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] them once when he had been lost in dreams. At the time he had put no faith
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in those dreams and was only tantalising himself by their hideous but daring
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] recklessness. Now, a month later, he had begun to look upon them
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] differently, and, in spite of the monologues in which he jeered at his own
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] impotence and indecision, he had involuntarily come to regard this
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “hideous” dream as an exploit to be attempted, although he still did not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] realise this himself. He was positively going now for a “rehearsal” of his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] project, and at every step his excitement grew more and more violent.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] With a sinking heart and a nervous tremor, he went up to a huge house
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which on one side looked on to the canal, and on the other into the street.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This house was let out in tiny tenements and was inhabited by working
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] people of all kinds—tailors, locksmiths, cooks, Germans of sorts, girls
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] picking up a living as best they could, petty clerks, etc. There was a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] continual coming and going through the two gates and in the two courtyards
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of the house. Three or four door-keepers were employed on the building.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The young man was very glad to meet none of them, and at once slipped
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unnoticed through the door on the right, and up the staircase. It was a back
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] staircase, dark and narrow, but he was familiar with it already, and knew his

[PAGE 14]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] way, and he liked all these surroundings: in such darkness even the most
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inquisitive eyes were not to be dreaded.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “If I am so scared now, what would it be if it somehow came to pass
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that I were really going to do it?” he could not help asking himself as he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] reached the fourth storey. There his progress was barred by some porters
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] who were engaged in moving furniture out of a flat. He knew that the flat
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] had been occupied by a German clerk in the civil service, and his family.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] This German was moving out then, and so the fourth floor on this staircase
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] would be untenanted except by the old woman. “That’s a good thing
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] anyway,” he thought to himself, as he rang the bell of the old woman’s flat.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The bell gave a faint tinkle as though it were made of tin and not of copper.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The little flats in such houses always have bells that ring like that. He had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] forgotten the note of that bell, and now its peculiar tinkle seemed to remind
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] him of something and to bring it clearly before him…. He started, his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] nerves were terribly overstrained by now. In a little while, the door was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] opened a tiny crack: the old woman eyed her visitor with evident distrust
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] through the crack, and nothing could be seen but her little eyes, glittering in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the darkness. But, seeing a number of people on the landing, she grew
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bolder, and opened the door wide. The young man stepped into the dark
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] entry, which was partitioned off from the tiny kitchen. The old woman
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stood facing him in silence and looking inquiringly at him. She was a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] diminutive, withered up old woman of sixty, with sharp malignant eyes and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] a sharp little nose. Her colourless, somewhat grizzled hair was thickly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] smeared with oil, and she wore no kerchief over it. Round her thin long
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] neck, which looked like a hen’s leg, was knotted some sort of flannel rag,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and, in spite of the heat, there hung flapping on her shoulders, a mangy fur
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cape, yellow with age. The old woman coughed and groaned at every
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] instant. The young man must have looked at her with a rather peculiar
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] expression, for a gleam of mistrust came into her eyes again.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Raskolnikov, a student, I came here a month ago,” the young man
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] made haste to mutter, with a half bow, remembering that he ought to be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] more polite.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I remember, my good sir, I remember quite well your coming here,”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the old woman said distinctly, still keeping her inquiring eyes on his face.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “And here… I am again on the same errand,” Raskolnikov continued, a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] little disconcerted and surprised at the old woman’s mistrust. “Perhaps she

[PAGE 15]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] is always like that though, only I did not notice it the other time,” he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] thought with an uneasy feeling.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The old woman paused, as though hesitating; then stepped on one side,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and pointing to the door of the room, she said, letting her visitor pass in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] front of her:
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Step in, my good sir.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The little room into which the young man walked, with yellow paper on
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the walls, geraniums and muslin curtains in the windows, was brightly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] lighted up at that moment by the setting sun.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “So the sun will shine like this then too!” flashed as it were by chance
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] through Raskolnikov’s mind, and with a rapid glance he scanned everything

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] arrangement. But there was nothing special in the room. The furniture, all
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] very old and of yellow wood, consisted of a sofa with a huge bent wooden
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] back, an oval table in front of the sofa, a dressing-table with a looking-glass
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fixed on it between the windows, chairs along the walls and two or three
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] half-penny prints in yellow frames, representing German damsels with birds
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in their hands—that was all. In the corner a light was burning before a small
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ikon. Everything was very clean; the floor and the furniture were brightly

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Lizaveta’s work,” thought the young man. There was not a speck of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] dust to be seen in the whole flat.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “It’s in the houses of spiteful old widows that one finds such
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cleanliness,” Raskolnikov thought again, and he stole a curious glance at
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the cotton curtain over the door leading into another tiny room, in which
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stood the old woman’s bed and chest of drawers and into which he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] never looked before. These two rooms made up the whole flat.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What do you want?” the old woman said severely, coming into the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] room and, as before, standing in front of him so as to look him straight in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the face.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I’ve brought something to pawn here,” and he drew out of his pocket
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] an old-fashioned flat silver watch, on the back of which was engraved a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] globe; the chain was of steel.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “But the time is up for your last pledge. The month was up the day
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] before yesterday.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “I will bring you the interest for another month; wait a little.”

[PAGE 16]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “But that’s for me to do as I please, my good sir, to wait or to sell your
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pledge at once.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “How much will you give me for the watch, Alyona Ivanovna?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “You come with such trifles, my good sir, it’s scarcely worth anything. I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] gave you two roubles last time for your ring and one could buy it quite new

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Give me four roubles for it, I shall redeem it, it was my father’s. I shall

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “A rouble and a half, and interest in advance, if you like!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “A rouble and a half!” cried the young man.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Please yourself”—and the old woman handed him back the watch. The

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The old woman fumbled in her pocket for her keys, and disappeared

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] unlocking the chest of drawers.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “It must be the top drawer,” he reflected. “So she carries the keys in a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pocket on the right. All in one bunch on a steel ring…. And there’s one key
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] there, three times as big as all the others, with deep notches; that can’t be
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the key of the chest of drawers… then there must be some other chest or
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] strong-box… that’s worth knowing. Strong-boxes always have keys like
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that… but how degrading it all is.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The old woman came back.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Here, sir: as we say ten copecks the rouble a month, so I must take
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] fifteen copecks from a rouble and a half for the month in advance. But for

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] same reckoning in advance. That makes thirty-five copecks altogether. So I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] must give you a rouble and fifteen copecks for the watch. Here it is.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What! only a rouble and fifteen copecks now!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Just so.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The young man did not dispute it and took the money. He looked at the

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Good-bye—are you always at home alone, your sister is not here with

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “What business is she of yours, my good sir?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Oh, nothing particular, I simply asked. You are too quick…. Good-day,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Alyona Ivanovna.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov went out in complete confusion. This confusion became

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] exclamations, could express his agitation. The feeling of intense repulsion,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] which had begun to oppress and torture his heart while he was on his way to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the old woman, had by now reached such a pitch and had taken such a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] definite form that he did not know what to do with himself to escape from
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his wretchedness. He walked along the pavement like a drunken man,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] regardless of the passers-by, and jostling against them, and only came to his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] senses when he was in the next street. Looking round, he noticed that he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was standing close to a tavern which was entered by steps leading from the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] pavement to the basement. At that instant two drunken men came out at the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] door, and abusing and supporting one another, they mounted the steps.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Without stopping to think, Raskolnikov went down the steps at once. Till
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that moment he had never been into a tavern, but now he felt giddy and was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] tormented by a burning thirst. He longed for a drink of cold beer, and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] attributed his sudden weakness to the want of food. He sat down at a sticky
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] little table in a dark and dirty corner; ordered some beer, and eagerly drank
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] off the first glassful. At once he felt easier; and his thoughts became clear.

[PAGE 18]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “All that’s nonsense,” he said hopefully, “and there is nothing in it all to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] worry about! It’s simply physical derangement. Just a glass of beer, a piece
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of dry bread—and in one moment the brain is stronger, the mind is clearer
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and the will is firm! Phew, how utterly petty it all is!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But in spite of this scornful reflection, he was by now looking cheerful
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] as though he were suddenly set free from a terrible burden: and he gazed
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] round in a friendly way at the people in the room. But even at that moment
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he had a dim foreboding that this happier frame of mind was also not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] normal.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] There were few people at the time in the tavern. Besides the two
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] drunken men he had met on the steps, a group consisting of about five men
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and a girl with a concertina had gone out at the same time. Their departure
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] left the room quiet and rather empty. The persons still in the tavern were a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] man who appeared to be an artisan, drunk, but not extremely so, sitting
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] before a pot of beer, and his companion, a huge, stout man with a grey
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] beard, in a short full-skirted coat. He was very drunk: and had dropped
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] asleep on the bench; every now and then, he began as though in his sleep,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cracking his fingers, with his arms wide apart and the upper part of his body
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bounding about on the bench, while he hummed some meaningless refrain,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] trying to recall some such lines as these:
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “His wife a year he fondly loved His wife a—a year he—fondly loved.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Or suddenly waking up again:
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Walking along the crowded row He met the one he used to know.”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But no one shared his enjoyment: his silent companion looked with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] positive hostility and mistrust at all these manifestations. There was another
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] man in the room who looked somewhat like a retired government clerk. He
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was sitting apart, now and then sipping from his pot and looking round at
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the company. He, too, appeared to be in some agitation.

[PAGE 19]
[L s=25.0 r=1.67 x=221 y=113 b=0 i=0 f=f1] Chapter Two
[L s=27.5 r=1.83 x=77 y=170 b=0 i=0 f=f3] R
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=317 y=179 b=0 i=0 f=f3] , and, as we said before, he avoided
[L s=10.5 r=0.70 x=95 y=183 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ASKOLNIKOV WAS NOT USED TO CROWDS
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=201 b=0 i=0 f=f3] society of every sort, more especially of late. But now all at once he felt a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=219 b=0 i=0 f=f3] desire to be with other people. Something new seemed to be taking place
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=237 b=0 i=0 f=f3] within him, and with it he felt a sort of thirst for company. He was so weary
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=255 b=0 i=0 f=f3] after a whole month of concentrated wretchedness and gloomy excitement
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=273 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that he longed to rest, if only for a moment, in some other world, whatever
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=291 b=0 i=0 f=f3] it might be; and, in spite of the filthiness of the surroundings, he was glad
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=309 b=0 i=0 f=f3] now to stay in the tavern.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=327 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The master of the establishment was in another room, but he frequently
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=345 b=0 i=0 f=f3] came down some steps into the main room, his jaunty, tarred boots with red
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=363 b=0 i=0 f=f3] turn-over tops coming into view each time before the rest of his person. He
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=381 b=0 i=0 f=f3] wore a full coat and a horribly greasy black satin waistcoat, with no cravat,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=399 b=0 i=0 f=f3] and his whole face seemed smeared with oil like an iron lock. At the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=417 b=0 i=0 f=f3] counter stood a boy of about fourteen, and there was another boy somewhat
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=435 b=0 i=0 f=f3] younger who handed whatever was wanted. On the counter lay some sliced
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=453 b=0 i=0 f=f3] cucumber, some pieces of dried black bread, and some fish, chopped up
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=471 b=0 i=0 f=f3] small, all smelling very bad. It was insufferably close, and so heavy with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=489 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the fumes of spirits that five minutes in such an atmosphere might well
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=507 b=0 i=0 f=f3] make a man drunk.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=525 b=0 i=0 f=f3] There are chance meetings with strangers that interest us from the first
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=543 b=0 i=0 f=f3] moment, before a word is spoken. Such was the impression made on
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=561 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov by the person sitting a little distance from him, who looked
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=579 b=0 i=0 f=f3] like a retired clerk. The young man often recalled this impression
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=597 b=0 i=0 f=f3] afterwards, and even ascribed it to presentiment. He looked repeatedly at
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=615 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the clerk, partly no doubt because the latter was staring persistently at him,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=633 b=0 i=0 f=f3] obviously anxious to enter into conversation. At the other persons in the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=651 b=0 i=0 f=f3] room, including the tavern-keeper, the clerk looked as though he were used
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=669 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to their company, and weary of it, showing a shade of condescending
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=687 b=0 i=0 f=f3] contempt for them as persons of station and culture inferior to his own, with

[PAGE 20]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] whom it would be useless for him to converse. He was a man over fifty,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] bald and grizzled, of medium height, and stoutly built. His face, bloated
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] from continual drinking, was of a yellow, even greenish, tinge, with swollen
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] eyelids out of which keen reddish eyes gleamed like little chinks. But there
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] was something very strange in him; there was a light in his eyes as though
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of intense feeling—perhaps there were even thought and intelligence, but at
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the same time there was a gleam of something like madness. He was
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] wearing an old and hopelessly ragged black dress coat, with all its buttons
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] missing except one, and that one he had buttoned, evidently clinging to this
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] last trace of respectability. A crumpled shirt front, covered with spots and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stains, protruded from his canvas waistcoat. Like a clerk, he wore no beard,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] nor moustache, but had been so long unshaven that his chin looked like a
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] stiff greyish brush. And there was something respectable and like an official
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] about his manner too. But he was restless; he ruffled up his hair and from
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] time to time let his head drop into his hands dejectedly resting his ragged
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] elbows on the stained and sticky table. At last he looked straight at
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Raskolnikov, and said loudly and resolutely:
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “May I venture, honoured sir, to engage you in polite conversation?
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Forasmuch as, though your exterior would not command respect, my
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] experience admonishes me that you are a man of education and not
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] accustomed to drinking. I have always respected education when in
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] conjunction with genuine sentiments, and I am besides a titular counsellor
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] in rank. Marmeladov—such is my name; titular counsellor. I make bold to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] inquire—have you been in the service?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “No, I am studying,” answered the young man, somewhat surprised at
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the grandiloquent style of the speaker and also at being so directly
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] addressed. In spite of the momentary desire he had just been feeling for
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] company of any sort, on being actually spoken to he felt immediately his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] habitual irritable and uneasy aversion for any stranger who approached or
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] attempted to approach him.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “A student then, or formerly a student,” cried the clerk. “Just what I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] thought! I’m a man of experience, immense experience, sir,” and he tapped
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] his forehead with his fingers in self-approval. “You’ve been a student or
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] have attended some learned institution!… But allow me….” He got up,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] staggered, took up his jug and glass, and sat down beside the young man,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=704 b=0 i=0 f=f3] facing him a little sideways. He was drunk, but spoke fluently and boldly,

[PAGE 21]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] only occasionally losing the thread of his sentences and drawling his words.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He pounced upon Raskolnikov as greedily as though he too had not spoken
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to a soul for a month.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Honoured sir,” he began almost with solemnity, “poverty is not a vice,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that’s a true saying. Yet I know too that drunkenness is not a virtue, and that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] that’s even truer. But beggary, honoured sir, beggary is a vice. In poverty
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] you may still retain your innate nobility of soul, but in beggary—never—no
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] one. For beggary a man is not chased out of human society with a stick, he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] is swept out with a broom, so as to make it as humiliating as possible; and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] quite right, too, forasmuch as in beggary I am ready to be the first to
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] humiliate myself. Hence the pot-house! Honoured sir, a month ago Mr.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Lebeziatnikov gave my wife a beating, and my wife is a very different
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] matter from me! Do you understand? Allow me to ask you another question
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] out of simple curiosity: have you ever spent a night on a hay barge, on the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Neva?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “No, I have not happened to,” answered Raskolnikov. “What do you
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] mean?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Well, I’ve just come from one and it’s the fifth night I’ve slept so….”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He filled his glass, emptied it and paused. Bits of hay were in fact clinging
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to his clothes and sticking to his hair. It seemed quite probable that he had
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] not undressed or washed for the last five days. His hands, particularly, were
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] filthy. They were fat and red, with black nails.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] His conversation seemed to excite a general though languid interest.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The boys at the counter fell to sniggering. The innkeeper came down from
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] the upper room, apparently on purpose to listen to the “funny fellow” and
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] sat down at a little distance, yawning lazily, but with dignity. Evidently
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Marmeladov was a familiar figure here, and he had most likely acquired his
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] weakness for high-flown speeches from the habit of frequently entering into
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] conversation with strangers of all sorts in the tavern. This habit develops
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] into a necessity in some drunkards, and especially in those who are looked
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=614 b=0 i=0 f=f3] after sharply and kept in order at home. Hence in the company of other
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=632 b=0 i=0 f=f3] drinkers they try to justify themselves and even if possible obtain
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] consideration.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Funny fellow!” pronounced the innkeeper. “And why don’t you work,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] why aren’t you at your duty, if you are in the service?”

[PAGE 22]
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Why am I not at my duty, honoured sir,” Marmeladov went on,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] addressing himself exclusively to Raskolnikov, as though it had been he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] who put that question to him. “Why am I not at my duty? Does not my heart
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=128 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ache to think what a useless worm I am? A month ago when Mr.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Lebeziatnikov beat my wife with his own hands, and I lay drunk, didn’t I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] suffer? Excuse me, young man, has it ever happened to you… hm… well,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=182 b=0 i=0 f=f3] to petition hopelessly for a loan?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=200 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Yes, it has. But what do you mean by hopelessly?”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Hopelessly in the fullest sense, when you know beforehand that you
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] will get nothing by it. You know, for instance, beforehand with positive
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] certainty that this man, this most reputable and exemplary citizen, will on
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] no consideration give you money; and indeed I ask you why should he? For
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] he knows of course that I shan’t pay it back. From compassion? But Mr.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Lebeziatnikov who keeps up with modern ideas explained the other day that
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] compassion is forbidden nowadays by science itself, and that that’s what is
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] done now in England, where there is political economy. Why, I ask you,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] should he give it to me? And yet though I know beforehand that he won’t, I
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] set off to him and…”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Why do you go?” put in Raskolnikov.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=416 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Well, when one has no one, nowhere else one can go! For every man
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] must have somewhere to go. Since there are times when one absolutely
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=452 b=0 i=0 f=f3] must go somewhere! When my own daughter first went out with a yellow
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=470 b=0 i=0 f=f3] ticket, then I had to go… (for my daughter has a yellow passport),” he
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=488 b=0 i=0 f=f3] added in parenthesis, looking with a certain uneasiness at the young man.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=506 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “No matter, sir, no matter!” he went on hurriedly and with apparent
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=524 b=0 i=0 f=f3] composure when both the boys at the counter guffawed and even the
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=542 b=0 i=0 f=f3] innkeeper smiled—“No matter, I am not confounded by the wagging of
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] their heads; for everyone knows everything about it already, and all that is
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=578 b=0 i=0 f=f3] secret is made open. And I accept it all, not with contempt, but with
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=596 b=0 i=0 f=f3] humility. So be it! So be it! ‘Behold the man!’ Excuse me, young man, can

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=650 b=0 i=0 f=f3] The young man did not answer a word.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=668 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Well,” the orator began again stolidly and with even increased dignity,
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=686 b=0 i=0 f=f3] after waiting for the laughter in the room to subside. “Well, so be it, I am a

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=74 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Ivanovna, my spouse, is a person of education and an officer’s daughter.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=92 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Granted, granted, I am a scoundrel, but she is a woman of a noble heart, full
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=110 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of sentiments, refined by education. And yet… oh, if only she felt for me!

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=146 b=0 i=0 f=f3] place where people feel for him! But Katerina Ivanovna, though she is
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=164 b=0 i=0 f=f3] magnanimous, she is unjust…. And yet, although I realise that when she

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=218 b=0 i=0 f=f3] dignity, hearing the sniggering again—“but, my God, if she would but
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=236 b=0 i=0 f=f3] once…. But no, no! It’s all in vain and it’s no use talking! No use talking!
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=254 b=0 i=0 f=f3] For more than once, my wish did come true and more than once she has felt
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=272 b=0 i=0 f=f3] for me but… such is my fate and I am a beast by nature!”
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Rather!” assented the innkeeper yawning. Marmeladov struck his fist
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=308 b=0 i=0 f=f3] resolutely on the table.
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=326 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Such is my fate! Do you know, sir, do you know, I have sold her very

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] of things, but her stockings, her stockings I have sold for drink! Her mohair

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=398 b=0 i=0 f=f3] mine; and we live in a cold room and she caught cold this winter and has

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=434 b=0 i=0 f=f3] Katerina Ivanovna is at work from morning till night; she is scrubbing and

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=99 y=560 b=0 i=0 f=f3] “Young man,” he went on, raising his head again, “in your face I seem

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=344 b=0 i=0 f=f3] He used to beat her at the end: and although she paid him back, of which I

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=290 b=0 i=0 f=f3] But it’s no use going over that! Sonia, as you may well fancy, has had no

[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=362 b=0 i=0 f=f3] hm, anyway we have not even those now, so all our instruction came to an
[L s=15.0 r=1.00 x=77 y=380 b=0 i=0 f=f3] end. We stopped at Cyrus of Persia. Since she has attained years of
